coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot inlaid carbon nanofibers: Revealing the edge activity for ultrahigh rate pseudocapacitive energy storage
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jiayao
Wang, Luxiang
Gan, Xuemeng
Tang, Tingting
Qin, Fuwei
Luo, Wanxia
Li, Qiqi
Guo, Nannan
Zhang, Su
Jia, Dianzeng
Song, Huaihe
coredata.dc:description: The edge sites of carbon show high electrochemical activity because of the unique electronic structure and functionalization. But revealing their energy storage ability and the underlying mechanism are still challenging. Herein, we prepare a novel edge-enriched carbon nanofiber fabric by exposing the embedded graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through partial hydrolysis of the electrospun GQDs/polyacrylonitrile fabric. The edges of inlaid GQDs are mainly terminated by various oxygen-bearing functional groups even after high temperature treatment, simultaneously contributing to abundant surface redox active sites and good electrolyte wettability. Further facilitated by the entire conductive networks, the edge sites are proved to show an ultrafast pseudocapacitive performance (200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, 130 F g−1 at 100 A g−1) because of the robust electrochemical kinetics. Moreover, at an ultra-high mass loading of 25.5 mg cm−2 (thickness: 1.4 mm), the multilayer-folded fabric performs a remarkable areal capacitance (3.95 F cm−2 at 25.5 mA cm−2), outstanding rate performance (2.04 F cm−2 at 510 mA cm−2), and good cycle stability (98% capacitance retention after 10,000 times), demonstrating the great potential of edge structure design for high power devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Edge sites
Graphene quantum dots
Fast charge-storage
Pseudocapacitance
Supercapacitors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52062046, 51702275, U1703251, and 52072326), the Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (2020D01C019), the Research Innovation Project of Postgraduate students in Xinjiang Autonomous Region (XJ2020G008), and the Xinjiang University Doctoral Research Foundation (XJUBSCX-201910).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) SEM images and (c) AFM image of GQD-CNF. (d and e) SEM images and (f) AFM image of Edge-CNF. The insets of (a) and (d) present the diameter distributions of the samples. (g and h) HRTEM image of Edge-CNF. (i) Photograph of Edge-CNF after folding and compression.

(a) Photographs of deionized water, hydrothermal treated PAN solution, and hydrothermal treated GQD/PAN solution. (b) FT-IR spectra of GQDs and the collection from hydrothermal treated GQD/PAN solution. (c) The mass residual of Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN after different reaction times. (d) FT-IR spectra, (e) XPS survey spectra, and (f) XPS C1s spectra of the Stabilized GQD/PAN and Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN. (g) The hydrolysis process of Stabilized GQD/PAN nanofibers.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) XPS survey spectra, (c) XPS C1s spectra, and (d) contribution of components in C1s spectra of the GQD-CNF, Hydro-CNF, and Edge-CNF.

(a) CV curves of GQD-CNF, Hydro-CNF, and Edge-CNF at 10 mV s−1. (b) CV curves and (c) GCD curves of Edge-CNF. (d) Specific capacitances at various current densities and (e) electrochemical kinetic parameters of GQD-CNF, Hydro-CNF, and Edge-CNF. (f) Schematic illustration of the pseudocapacitive energy storage of Edge-CNF.

Electrochemical performance of Edge-CNF//Edge-CNF symmetric supercapacitors: (a) Scheme of the multilayer-folded Edge-CNF as the thick electrodes. (b) CV curves at 100 mV s−1 with different mass loadings. Comparison of (c) the electrode areal capacitance and (d) Ragone plots with other carbon-based supercapacitors. (e) Cycling stability of the device at a mass commercial loading of 10 mg cm−2 at 10 A g−1.

Schematic illustration of the sample preparation procedures: the GQD embedded PAN nanofibers are first prepared by electrospinning using a GQDs and PAN mixed spinning solution. Edge-CNF is prepared through subsequent thermal stabilization, hydrothermal treatment, and carbonization. For comparison, GQD-CNF is prepared through the similar procedure as Edge-CNF but without hydrothermal treatment. Hydro-CNF is also prepared through electrospinning of pure PAN spinning solution, thermal stabilization, hydrothermal treatment, and carbonization.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot inlaid carbon nanofibers: Revealing the edge activity for ultrahigh rate pseudocapacitive energy storage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Institute of Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Autonomous Region, Urumqi 830046, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources
Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aState Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Institute of Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Autonomous Region, Urumqi 830046, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resources Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resources Engineering
Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory of Chemical Resources Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The edge sites of carbon show high electrochemical activity because of the unique electronic structure and functionalization. But revealing their energy storage ability and the underlying mechanism are still challenging. Herein, we prepare a novel edge-enriched carbon nanofiber fabric by exposing the embedded graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through partial hydrolysis of the electrospun GQDs/polyacrylonitrile fabric. The edges of inlaid GQDs are mainly terminated by various oxygen-bearing functional groups even after high temperature treatment, simultaneously contributing to abundant surface redox active sites and good electrolyte wettability. Further facilitated by the entire conductive networks, the edge sites are proved to show an ultrafast pseudocapacitive performance (200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, 130 F g−1 at 100 A g−1) because of the robust electrochemical kinetics. Moreover, at an ultra-high mass loading of 25.5 mg cm−2 (thickness: 1.4 mm), the multilayer-folded fabric performs a remarkable areal capacitance (3.95 F cm−2 at 25.5 mA cm−2), outstanding rate performance (2.04 F cm−2 at 510 mA cm−2), and good cycle stability (98% capacitance retention after 10,000 times), demonstrating the great potential of edge structure design for high power devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials have attracted extensive attention as electrode materials for supercapacitors owing to the low-cost, good physicochemical stability, and high conductivity. They usually show an electrical double-layer energy storage behavior with robust ion adsorption/desorption kinetics, which mainly depends on the specific surface area and porous structure [1–5]. However, the electrical double-layer property contributes to a rather low intrinsic capacitance per surface area (e.g., 0.21 μF cm−2 for ideal graphene) [6]. As in-pore ion migration is the primary rate controlling process, deep and tortuous porous structure usually results in sluggish electrochemical kinetics. More seriously, the ion migration channels are continuously blocked with the increase of electrode thickness or mass loadings, resulting in the rapid fading of the capacitance and rate performance that cannot meet the requirements for future high energy and power devices [7,8]. Therefore, designing new active sites with high storage capability, robust electrochemical kinetics, and good cycle stability for high mass loading electrodes is of great importance.

For carbon materials, surface heteroatoms and functional groups are widely adopted as the pseudocapacitive sites in aqueous electrolyte due to their good redox activity [9–14]. Recently, we found that self-doping defects generated by ball-milling could also act as the active sites for capacitive energy storage, showing a robust electrical double-layer like behavior [15]. However, introducing these active sites in the bulk phase of carbon inevitably destroys the continuous electron transport paths, resulting in the decreased rate performance [2]. The edge sites of carbon show some novel properties such as similar electronic structure as some of the self-doping defects, high reactivity, and easy functionalization. Studied by an ideal graphene model, previous work shows that the edges can gather more electrons, thus providing a four orders of magnitude higher capacitance than its basal plane [16–18]. Thus, using the exposed edges of carbon for capacitive energy storage can provide diffusion-free active sites without destroying its continuous conductive networks, which is of potential for high energy-power density devices [19–21]. However, current research is mostly limited to reveal the capability of edge sites through ideal model products (e.g., graphene) or molecular simulation. The edge sites are usually terminated by C-H or various oxygen-bearing functional groups in practice, leading to large deviation from the model product. In addition, most large-sized carbon materials have insufficient edge sites, resulting in a limited capacitance contribution. Thus, there are still huge challenges to reveal the underlying mechanism and make efficient utilization of the edge sites of carbon materials for high-performance supercapacitors.

The small-sized graphene quantum dots (GQDs, lamella size<10 nm) have more edge sites compared to other carbon materials, showing great potential for revealing the edge activity [22–26]. Herein, we present a hydrothermal hydrolysis strategy to expose the edge sites of GQDs inlaid in carbon nanofibers for high performance supercapacitors (Scheme 1 ). First, GQDs are homogeneously embedded into the polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers by electrospinning and thermal stabilization [27]. Then, a novel edge-enriched carbon nanofiber fabric (Edge-CNF) is prepared through partial hydrolysis of PAN and subsequent carbonization. The edges are mainly terminated by oxygen-bearing functional groups, which contributes to abundant surface redox active sites and good electrolyte wettability. Moreover, the embedded GQDs construct entire conductive networks inside the carbon nanofibers, facilitating the fast pseudocapacitive kinetics. Therefore, the as-prepared Edge-CNF with an ultrahigh mass loading of 25.5 mg cm−2 achieves a high areal capacitance (3.95 F cm−2 at 25.5 mA cm−2), outstanding rate capability (2.04 F cm−2 at 510 mA cm−2), and good cycle stability, which are superior to most of the reported carbon fabrics. The device can deliver an ultrahigh areal energy density of 70.8 µWh cm−2 under the power density of 127.5 mW cm−2, revealing great potential for practical application.

GQDs are prepared by chemical oxidation according to our previous works [28,29]. The decoration of abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups enables their good dispersibility in the spinning solution. Therefore, GQDs are completely embedded in the PAN nanofibers during electrospinning. After subsequnt thermal stabilization (Stabilized GQD/PAN) and carbonization, the GQD-reinforced carbon nanofibers (GQD-CNFs) show a smooth surface structure and an average diameter of 434 nm from the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Fig. 1 a and b) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Fig. 1c) images [27]. But when the Stabilized GQD/PAN nanofibers are hydrothermally treated before carbonization, we realize that the average diameter obviously decreases to 238 nm of Edge-CNFs (Fig. 1d) because of the gradual hydrolysis and removal of the PAN outer layers (the details are seen below). What's more, Edge-CNFs show a rough surface with many nano-dots inlaid on the nanofibers (Fig. 1e and f). This phenomenon clearly demonstrates that by removing the PAN outer layers, the edge sites of the embedded GQDs are exposed to the surface. As further confirmed by high-transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images, the rough and etched surface of Edge-CNFs (Fig. 1g) is enriched with highly crystallized domains with a lattice parameter of 0.243 nm (Fig. 1h), corresponding to the (1
1 ¯
00) crystal face of the GQD basal plane [15,30]. It is also realized that the nano-dots (ca. 30 nm) are much larger than the primary GQDs (average 3.2 nm). This is because some of the PAN molecules are strongly aggregated and cross-linked on GQDs thanks to the good chemical reactivity. Thus these clusters are remained without falling-off during hydrothermal process (Fig. S1) [27,31]. What's more, the Edge-CNF fabric still maintains good mechanical strength (the Young's modulus is 1.26 MPa, Fig. S2) and flexibility after hydrothermal treatment. It can recover to its initial shape after cyclic compression and folding (Fig. 1i), suggesting great potential for multilayer foldable electrode materials.

To clearly explain the formation of the edge-enriched structure of Edge-CNF, we first investigated the hydrolysis process. The thermal stabilized PAN nanofibers and GQDs/PAN composite nanofibers are denoted as Stabilized PAN and Stabilized GQD/PAN, respectively. The corresponding samples after hydrothermal process are named as Hydrothermal-treated PAN and Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN. The residual solution of Hydrothermal-treated PAN (middle in Fig. 2 a) is straw yellow compared with deionized water (left in Fig. 2a), revealing that some soluble molecules are formed through partial hydrolysis of the PAN nanofibers [22,32]. The residual solution of Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN shows a dark red color (right in Fig. 2a) similar to pure GQDs solution (Fig. S3). Furthermore, we measured the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the dried Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN residual. The nitrogen-bearing groups such as -CN (2240 cm−1), -C=N- (1584 cm−1), and -C-N (1430 cm−1) are primarily coming from the soluble molecules by hydrolysis of PAN; and the oxygen-bearing groups such as -COOH/-OH (3200 cm−1), -C=O (1720 cm−1), and C-O-C (1250 cm−1) are from the released GQDs (Fig. 2b) [29,33]. The above results demonstrate that some of the embedded GQDs are dissolved into the solution along with the hydrolysis of PAN, leading to a continuous mass loss (19% after 10 h, Fig. 2c).

From the FT-IR spectra of the solid samples (Fig. 2d), Stabilized GQD/PAN and Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN contain similar functional groups such as C-O-C (1250 cm−1), -C=C-/-C=N- (1590 cm−1), and -C-H (1370 cm−1) [34,35]. However, the -CN peak of Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN located at 2240 cm−1 is almost disappeared, indicating the removal of un-cyclized PAN components. Moreover, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra (Fig. 2e) show that after hydrothermal treatment, the N content significantly decreases from 8 at.% to 3 at.% with the increase of O content from 10 at.% to 20 at.%. From the XPS C1s spectra (Fig. 2f), the C-N/C-O component decreases from 20 at.% of Stabilized GQD/PAN to 14 at.% of Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN, and the C=O mainly from GQDs increases from 8 to 11 at.%. Based on the above, the hydrolysis of PAN and exposure of GQD edges on the surface of carbon nanofibers are shown in Fig. 2g. The surface functional groups of nanofibers, e.g. -CN and -C=N-, are hydrolyzed to generate soluble molecules, simultaneously exposing GQDs with high crystallinity and rich edge regions.

Next, the structure and chemical compositions of the obtained carbon nanofiber fabrics were characterized to investigate the property of edge sites. The carbon nanofiber fabric prepared through the similar procedure as Edge-CNF but without adding GQDs in the spinning solution (Hydro-CNF, Scheme 1) is also investigated to understand the effect of hydrothermal treatment. GQD-CNF shows an amorphous structure with no obvious diffraction peaks in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 3 a). By contrast, both Edge-CNF and Hydro-CNF show an obviously increased intensity of the (002) peak located at 24°, demonstrating that hydrothermal treatment significantly increases the crystallinity of the carbon nanofibers. As further proved by the Raman spectra (Fig. S4), both Hydro-CNF (1.58) and Edge-CNF (1.53) show lower AD/AG ratio than GQD-CNF (1.70), indicating the reduced defect density [36,37]. As shown in Fig. 2g, PAN molecules go through condensation cyclization during thermal stabilization, leading to the formation of conjugated six-member rings [38,39]. Although this structure is favorable to form highly crystallized graphitic lattice, certain amount of -CN groups are inevitably remained due to insufficient cyclization, which may distort the graphitic lattice and cause the formation of amorphous carbon during carbonization. As confirmed from the FT-IR and XPS results, most of the un-cyclized components containing -CN groups are removed during hydrolysis. Therefore, large area conjugated structure is formed in Edge-CNF, leading to a significantly improved conductivity from 18.7 S m−1 of GQD-CNF to 67.9 S m−1 of Edge-CNF (Fig. S5). Moreover, compared with the hydrothermal treated samples, the electrical conductivity of Edge-CNF is 9 times higher than that of Hydro-CNF (7.5 S m−1), demonstrating that the embedded GQDs with high crystallinity can significantly improve the electrical conductivity due to the formation of conductive network inside the nanofibers [27,31].

From the XPS survey spectra (Fig. 3b), Edge-CNF with enriched edges shows a more than 2 times higher O/C ratio of 0.26 compared with Hydro-CNF (0.13) and GQD-CNF (0.12). What's more, even after high temperature carbonization, the oxygen content only decreases from 22 at.% of the precursor Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN to 20 at.% of Edge-CNF. But for the samples without hydrothermal treatment, the oxygen content largely decreases from 20 at.% of the precursor Stabilized GQD/PAN to 10 at.% of GQD-CNF (Table S1). The result demonstrates that high oxygen-functionalization of Edge-CNF is mainly originated from the exposed edge sites of the inlaid GQDs. For clear understanding, the core level of C1s spectra are investigated in detail. The C1s spectra are deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6, 285.8, 286.8, and 288.3 eV, representing C=C, C=C-(O), C-O/C-N, and C=O components, respectively (Fig. 3c) [40]. It is reported that C-O-C mainly exists on the basal plane of graphitic lattice, but C=O usually decorates at the edge sites [41]. Therefore, from the structural feature, the obviously increased C=O from 1% to 10% of Edge-CNF further proves the exposure of GQD edges (Fig. 3d and Table S1). Furthermore, C=O components (e.g. carbonyl and quinones) are of high polarity and electrochemical activity in alkaline electrolyte, enabling significantly improved electrolyte wettability (Fig. S6) and abundant pseudocapacitive energy storage sites for Ed-CNF [10,42]. The contribution of oxygen-bearing functional groups to the pseudocapacitance can be attributed to the following reversible redox reactions [9,43]:
(1)
> C = O
+
e −
+
K +
> C − O K
(2)
C −
O −
+
K +
C − O K

The electrochemical performance of the samples is first studied using three-electrode system. All the samples show distorted cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at 10 mV s−1 from a rectangular shape (Fig. 4 a), indicating that their energy storage is combined by electrical double-layer and pseudocapacitive behaviors. Edge-CNF with enriched edge sites and functional groups shows the largest enclosed area and the most pronounced redox humps from -0.2 to -0.8 V vs. Hg/HgO reference among the samples. The surface area of carbon materials contributes a theoretical electrical double layer capacitance of 10–21 µF cm−2 [6]. As Edge-CNF has a specific surface area of 300 m2 g−1 (Fig. S7 and Table S2), it provides a calculated electrical double layer capacitance of 30–63 F g−1, which is far lower than the measured capacitance (200 F g−1). The result suggests that most capacitance of Edge-CNF is the pseudocapacitance originated from the multi-type edge sites and functional groups as energy storage sites, which is different from other carbon-based electrodes reported previously (Table S3). As a direct evidence, Edge-CNF shows almost no capacitance in organic electrolyte (Et4NBF4/PC) from the CV curves shown in Fig. S8 [13]. This is because the pseudocapacitive reactions by the exposed edges and oxygen- and/or nitrogen- bearing functional groups are barely occurred in organic electrolyte.

Apart from the edges and oxygen-bearing functional groups mentioned above, the doped nitrogen in carbon matrix also contribute to the pseudocapacitive sites in aqueous electrolytes [44]. As shown in Fig. 3b and Table S1, GQD-CNF shows a high nitrogen doping content of 8 at.% coming from the pyrolysis of PAN, leading to a capacitance of 85 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 even with a relatively low surface area of 77 m2 g−1 (Figs. 4d and S7). Benefitting from removal of the un-cyclized -CN component during hydrothermal process, Hydro-CNF derived from a more stable six-member ring networks (Hydrothermal-treated PAN) shows an even higher nitrogen content of 11 at.% than GQD-CNF, leading to increased capacitances at small current densities. The decreased rate performance of Hydro-CNF is due to the poor conductivity without GQDs. However, Edge-CNF after hydrothermal treatment shows a drastically decreased nitrogen content to only 3 at.%. This is because abundant edge sites from the inlaid GQDs occupy large portion of the Edge-CNF surface, leading to a less exposure of PAN derived nitrogen-doped carbon (Fig. 1e and f). As the small amount of doped nitrogen contributes to very limited capacitance, the edge sites and decorated oxygen-bearing functional groups are the main active sites for Edge-CNF.

Notably, the CV curves of Edge-CNF are almost overlapped even at a very high scan rate of 500 mV s−1 (Fig. 4b). Its galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves show high symmetry even at the high current density of 100 A g−1 with a rather low voltage drop of 0.158 V, indicating an extremely high rate performance (Fig. 4c). As shown in Fig. 4d, Edge-CNF shows 200, 174, 150, and 130 F g−1 at the current densities of 1, 10, 50, and 100 A g−1, which are much higher than those of GQD-CNF and Hydro-CNF. We have prepared the edge-enriched carbon nanofiber fabrics at different hydrothermal temperatures. XPS C1s spectra (Fig. S9) show that the C=O content gradually increase from 1 at.% to 12 at.%, indicating the gradually exposed edge sites. As seen from Fig. S10, the capacitance first increases with the increasing of hydrothermal temperature to the optimized 140 °C. Then, severe hydrolysis at 170 °C results in a partially destroyed conductive network as well as decreased capacitance and electrochemical kinetics. Thus, a reasonable edge site design is an effective method to improve the energy storage ability of carbon materials. Volumetric capacitance is another important parameter representing the performance of electrode materials. Edge-CNF has a high compression density of 0.92 g cm−3 (Fig. S11), leading to a good volumetric performance of 184 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 and 120 F cm−3 at 100 A g−1(Fig. S12).

Our work demonstrates that the edge sites and decorated functional groups having a pseudocapacitive behavior still show a very fast charge-storage ability, which is much different from the common view that pseudocapacitive materials show sluggish kinetics and poor rate performance [45]. To reveal the underlying mechanism, we measured the electrochemical kinetics of the samples by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, Fig. S13). As expected, Edge-CNF shows two or three times smaller charge-transfer and Warburg resistances than Hydro-CNF and GQD-CNF (Fig. 4e). Beside, we calculated the contribution of surface-controlled and diffusion-controlled capacitances, as shown in Fig. S14. According to the power-law correlation formula, the b-value (power-law index) of Edge-CNF in the operating voltage range is calculated to be between 0.9 and 1 [46]. The contribution of surface-controlled capacitance is 89% of the total capacitance [47], demonstrating a surface-controlled electrochemical response rather than a diffusion-controlled process. Therefore, the outstanding rate performance is ascribed to the multilevel structural properties from atomic to nano-micro scales (Fig. 3f): (1) Exposing the edge sites of the inlaid GQDs enables abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups on the surface even after high temperature treatment, not only providing abundant diffusion-free electrochemical active sites, but also ensuring good electrolyte wettability. (2) Embedding highly crystallized GQDs and hydrolysis of un-cyclized PAN construct entire conductive networks in Edge-CNF nanofibers, improving the electrical conductivity for more than 3.5 times. (3) The novel textile structure of Edge-CNF with macropores between the nanofibers provides large exposed surface area and abundant ion migration channels. Beneficial from the comprehensive merits, Edge-CNF shows high energy storage ability, robust electron and ion transport kinetics, as well as remarkable rate performance.

The good mechanical strength and flexibility, high conductivity, high hydrophilicity, and outstanding electrochemical performance also endow Edge-CNF great potential for practical application. As a proof of concept, we assembled the Edge-CNF//Edge-CNF symmetric supercapacitors with different mass loadings from 1.5 to 25.5 mg cm−2 (based on the area of the single electrode) by using simply multilayer-folded Edge-CNF as the electrodes (Fig. 5 a). The CV and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of the Edge-CNF//Edge-CNF supercapacitor show a quasi-rectangular and an isosceles triangular shape, respectively, demonstrating stable charge and discharge process (Fig. S15). The CV curves with difference mass loadings of the devices are almost overlapped even at high scan rate of 100 mV s−1 (Fig. 5b), demonstrating that the outstanding capacitive performance can be maintained at high rate and/or high mass loadings. Moreover, the areal capacitance increases linearly as the active mass loading increases (Fig. S16). The gravimetric capacitance and areal capacitance based on the electrodes and the devices are shown in Fig. S17. At 25.5 mg cm−2 (thickness: 1.4 mm), the electrode areal capacitances are up to 3.95 and 2.04 F cm−2 at the current densities of 25.5 and 510 mA cm−2, respectively. The rate performance is much better than other reported high-mass-loading carbon-based electrode materials (Fig. 5c) [48–53], as further indicated by the slightly increased relaxation time constants (τ0) with the increased mass loadings (Fig. S18). Therefore, the device shows high energy densities of 70.83 and 137.5 μWh cm−2 at the average power densities of 127.5 and 6.4 mW cm−2, respectively (Fig. 5d) [48–52]. Furthermore, the Edge-CNF//Edge-CNF supercapacitor (10 mg cm−2) displays good stability of ca. 98% capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1 (Fig. 5e). At 1 A g−1, the capacitance retention of the device after 10,000 cycles is ca. 100% (Fig. S19).

In summary, a novel edge-enriched carbon nanofiber fabric Edge-CNF is prepared by hydrothermal hydrolysis of GQD/PAN electrospun nanofiber. Partial peeling-off of the PAN skin leads to exposure of abundant edge sites of the inlaid GQDs. The active edge sites allow enriched oxygen-bearing functional groups even after high temperature treatment, which not only act as pseudocapacitive energy storage sites, but also improve the electrolyte wettability. The embedded GQDs construct entire conductive networks and reinforcing phase inside the carbon nanofibers, enabling high electrical conductivity, good mechanical strength, and high flexibility. In addition to the large exposed surface area and sufficient ion transport channels by the novel nanofiber structure, the as-prepared Edge-CNF shows a specific capacitance of 200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and an outstanding rate performance of 65% at 100 A g−1. Moreover, the multilayer-folded fabric at a very high mass loading of 25.5 mg cm−2 (thickness: 1.4 mm) delivers outstanding electrode areal capacitances 3.95 F cm−2 at 25.5 mA cm−2 and 2.04 F cm−2 even at an ultrahigh current density of 510 mA cm−2. Our work demonstrates that rational design of the edge structure is an effective method to improve the energy storage ability of carbon materials.
GQDs were prepared by chemical oxidation of the coal powder (Heishan, Xinjiang, China). The details are given in our previous studies.

Preparation of Edge-CNFs: 0.4 g PAN powder (PAN, J & K Scientific Co. Ltd., average molecular weight: 150,000 Da) and 0.2 g GQDs were completely dissolved in 5 mL dimethylformamide (AR) by vigorous stirring and sonication at 80 °C. The solution was then placed in a plastic syringe with a stainless steel needle (inner diameter: 1.12 mm). For electrospinning, a positive voltage of 18 kV and a negative voltage of -2 kV were applied to the spinneret and roller collector, respectively. The distance between the spinneret and the collector was fixed as 20 cm. The collector's scrolling speed was set as 80 rpm. For stabilization, the collected GQDs/PAN nanofibers were preoxidized at 280 °C in air for 2 h to obtain Stabilized GQD/PAN. The Stabilized GQD/PAN (5 × 5 cm) were immersed into 30 mL deionized water in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor (50 mL) and then heated at 140 °C for 6 h to obtain Hydrothermal-treated GQD/PAN. After cooling down to room temperature, the fabrics were washed using deionized water for three times then dried at 80 °C. Edge-CNF was obtained after carbonization at 800 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere. For comparison, Hydro-CNF was prepared through the same route but without adding GQDs in the electrospinning solution.
Preparation of GQD-CNFs: The synthetic method of GQD-CNFs was similar as that for Edge-CNFs but without the hydrothermal treatment.

The morphology and structure of the fabrics were characterized by SEM (Hitachi S-4800), HRTEM (JEOL-2100F), AFM (Bruker Dimension Icon), XRD (Bruker D8 Advances), FT-IR (Bruker VERTEX 70), XPS (ESCALAB 250 Xi), Raman spectroscopy (Bruker SENTERRA), and N2 physical adsorption-desorption isothermal method (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics). The contact angle toward electrolyte was measured using an optical contact angle tester (Kruss DSA100). The mechanical properties of the fabrics were measured using a universal mechanical tester (H5KT). The electric conductivity was measured from the I−V curves. The details are seen in our previous works [31].

For a three-electrode system, platinum sheet and standard Hg/HgO were used as the counter and the reference electrodes, respectively. The fabrics were directly used as the working electrode without using binders and conductive agents. To assemble two-electrode symmetric supercapacitors, the positive and negative electrodes were separated by the cellulose membrane (NKK TF-40) in the CR2032 lithium ion battery cell. Both three-electrodes and two-electrodes were tested using 6 mol L−1 KOH electrolyte. 1 mol L−1 tetraethylammonium tetrafluoroborate in propylene carbonate (Et4NBF4/PC) was used as organic electrolyte to assemble two-electrode organic-system supercapacitors. Electrochemical performance was evaluated by CV, GCD, and EIS using CHI660E electrochemical working station (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China). The frequency range and alternating voltage amplitude of the EIS measurement were set to be 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz and 5 mV, respectively.

The gravimetric capacitance (Cg , F g−1) was calculated from the charge-discharge curves using the following equation:
(3)
C g
=
I
Δ
t
m
Δ
U
Where I denotes the applied current, Δt the discharge time, m the individual electrode mass, and ΔU the potential range.

For two-electrode symmetric supercapacitor, the material capacitance (Cg ) and the cell capacitance (Ccell ) has a relationship:
(4)
C g
= 4
C
c e l l
considering the series relationship and the total mass of positive and negative electrodes.

The areal capacitance (Ca , F cm−2) and volumetric capacitance (Cv , F cm−3) are calculated from gravimetric capacitance according to the following equation:
(5)
C a
=
C g
×
m
(6)
C v
=
C g
×
ρ
Here, m (g cm−2) and ρ (g cm−3) are the areal mass loading and density of active materials, respectively.

The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) based on the electrode were calculated based on the following equations:
(7)
E =
C g
Δ
V 2
8
×
3.6
(8)
P =
3600 E
Δ
t
Where
Δ
V
(V) represents the voltage range of the device excluding the IR drop.

Jiayao Zhu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Methodology. Luxiang Wang: Formal analysis. Xuemeng Gan: Methodology. Tingting Tang: Methodology. Fuwei Qin: Methodology. Wanxia Luo: Formal analysis. Qiqi Li: Methodology. Nannan Guo: Formal analysis. Su Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology. Dianzeng Jia: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision. Huaihe Song: Formal analysis, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52062046, 51702275, U1703251, and 52072326), the Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (2020D01C019), the Research Innovation Project of Postgraduate students in Xinjiang Autonomous Region (XJ2020G008), and the Xinjiang University Doctoral Research Foundation (XJUBSCX-201910).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ensm.2022.02.015.
